# Why minimax × X+M collapses — pseudo MCP tool-call hallucination

*Generated by research-copilot.*

## The observation

| cell | minimax fa0 (1 seed, n=17) | task fails | pseudo MCP calls | real MCP calls |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Vanilla | 0.821 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| X+M | **0.392** | **8** | 3 (per failed task) | **0** |
| SE | 0.861 | 0 | (low) | (real, via geos-rag) |

X+M failed tasks: agent emits `<minimax:tool_call><invoke name="mcp__geos-rag__search_navigator">...</invoke></minimax:tool_call>` as **text** in the conversation, the runtime does not parse it as a tool call, agent gets no tool result, agent gives up at $\sim$25s with zero XMLs written.

## What was actually configured for X+M

Looking at the per-task artefacts in $/data/.../minimax\_minimax-m2.7/autocamp\_F4/.../TutorialSneddon/$:

\textbf{1. Tools the agent saw} (from the Claude Code system init event):

```
['Task', 'Bash', 'CronCreate', ..., 'Glob', 'Grep', 'NotebookEdit', 'Read',
 'TodoWrite', 'WebFetch', 'WebSearch', 'Write',
 'mcp__xmllint__validate_geos_xml']
```

Exactly one MCP tool: \texttt{mcp\_\_xmllint\_\_validate\_geos\_xml}.

\textbf{2. MCP servers connected} (from the same init event):

```
mcp_servers: [{'name': 'xmllint', 'status': 'connected'}]
```

Only the xmllint server. No \texttt{geos-rag} server.

\textbf{3. System prompt (the \texttt{--append-system-prompt} content)}:
- run/AGENTS.md (post-strip; harness contract: workspace paths, file rules, GEOSDATA path resolution)
- The 5-line GEOS contract primer ("\texttt{GEOS is an open-source multiphysics simulator. This task asks you to author one or more XML input files...}")
- A boilerplate anti-pseudo-tool-call instruction:

```
Use only real tool calls exposed by the runtime. Do not print XML-style
<invoke> blocks, <parameter> blocks, DSML function-call blocks, or
minimax:tool_call wrappers; those are text and will not execute.
```

\textbf{4. Memory cheatsheet} (m1u, prepended via \texttt{--append-system-prompt}). I grepped \texttt{plugin/memory\_primer\_dsv4\_m1u.md}, \texttt{plugin/GEOS\_PRIMER\_contract.md}, and \texttt{run/AGENTS.md} for any string matching \texttt{geos-rag|mcp\_\_geos|search\_navigator|search\_schema|search\_technical}: \textbf{zero matches}. The agent was given no in-prompt mention of any \texttt{mcp\_\_geos-rag\_\_*} tool name.

## So why does minimax invoke geos-rag?

\textbf{Answer: it's training-data prior, not in-prompt suggestion.}

Three pieces of evidence:

\textbf{(a) Vanilla minimax has zero pseudo MCP calls.} When no MCP server is registered (\texttt{mcp\_servers: []}), the agent's tool list contains only the standard tools (Bash, Read, etc.). minimax does not pseudo-call MCP tools in this regime. \texttt{tool\_calls.json} for the Vanilla failed task shows \texttt{pseudo\_tool\_calls: 0} and \texttt{per\_tool\_counts: \{Bash: 15, Read: 16, Write: 3, ...\}} (41 real calls).

\textbf{(b) X+M minimax pseudo-calls only \texttt{mcp\_\_geos-rag\_\_*}.} The hallucinated calls are specifically to \texttt{mcp\_\_geos-rag\_\_search\_navigator}, \texttt{search\_schema}, and \texttt{search\_technical} --- the tool names from our \texttt{plugin/.claude-plugin/plugin.json} \texttt{mcpServers} entry, which is a public artefact (it appears in our repo, in trajectories shared in DM-008 and similar docs, and in any open-sourced trace data the model may have been trained on). minimax has memorised the namespace.

\textbf{(c) The trigger is "any MCP tool in the tool list".} Vanilla → no MCP tool → no pseudo-calls. X+M → one MCP tool (\texttt{mcp\_\_xmllint\_\_validate\_geos\_xml}) → minimax fills in the rest of its remembered MCP namespace and emits text-formatted invocations. SE → multiple MCP tools (xmllint AND geos-rag) → minimax's pseudo-calls happen to land on real handlers, so the failure is hidden.

minimax's training distribution evidently includes Claude Code-style agent traces, and it pattern-matches "any \texttt{mcp\_\_*} tool present" → "the geos-rag-style suite must also be present". When the geos-rag suite is absent, minimax emits the calls as text rather than admitting it has nothing to call.

## Why DSv4-flash and gemini-3-flash-preview don't exhibit this

Two non-exclusive explanations:

\textbf{(1) Different training-data distribution.} DSv4-flash and gemini are trained on different corpora than minimax. They likely have less or no exposure to \texttt{mcp\_\_geos-rag\_\_*} as a strong attractor. Their priors over MCP tool names are not specifically tuned to our plugin's namespace.

\textbf{(2) Different tool-call discipline.} DSv4-flash in particular is rigorous about respecting the explicit tool list provided at session init --- it emits \texttt{tool\_use} blocks only for tools it sees in the list, not for tools it imagines might exist. Gemini behaves similarly. Minimax has a known weakness here: it generates "pseudo tool calls" --- XML-style or DSML-style text mock-ups that look like tool invocations but are just text in the assistant message. Our existing system-prompt instruction explicitly forbids this pattern (see \S above), but minimax ignores it on this configuration.

The question of whether (1) or (2) is dominant is empirically resolvable by trying a different MCP namespace (e.g., a one-off tool with a name minimax hasn't seen) and observing whether the pseudo-call still fires. We have not done this experiment.

## What this means for the cross-model story in the paper

The factor "X" in our autocamp factorial design was supposed to mean "agent-callable xmllint validator". On DSv4-flash this corresponds to "the agent has a single MCP tool, \texttt{mcp\_\_xmllint\_\_validate\_geos\_xml}, available alongside its native tool list". On minimax the same factor means "a single MCP tool, plus a 100\%-rate hallucination of three other MCP tools that don't exist". The factor is non-orthogonal to the underlying base-model's pretraining-induced MCP-namespace prior.

This is a cross-model finding worth reporting, and it has a concrete recommendation: \emph{when registering an MCP tool on a backbone with strong priors over a familiar MCP namespace, either register the entire expected namespace (even with stub handlers) or explicitly disambiguate in the system prompt}. The SE cell unwittingly does the former; the X+M cell does neither and pays the price.

## Proposed fix for the minimax × X+M re-run

Three options, in increasing intervention:

\textbf{Option A — explicit anti-hallucination prompt clause (lightest)}

Inject into the system prompt (via \texttt{--append-system-prompt} extra text) a specific instruction naming the geos-rag tools as unavailable:

\begin{verbatim}
NOTE on available MCP tools in this configuration:

The only MCP tool registered is `mcp__xmllint__validate_geos_xml`,
which validates XML against the GEOS schema.

The following MCP tools are NOT registered and NOT available:
- `mcp__geos-rag__search_navigator`
- `mcp__geos-rag__search_schema`
- `mcp__geos-rag__search_technical`

Do NOT call those tools (no `<minimax:tool_call>`, no XML-style
<invoke> wrappers, no DSML). They will not execute.

To find example XML files: use Glob/Grep/Read against /geos_lib/.
To validate XML: use mcp__xmllint__validate_geos_xml.
\end{verbatim}

This is targeted and reversible. It does not change the cell's semantics for DSv4 or gemini (those backbones already don't pseudo-call). It simply tells minimax explicitly what's available and what's not, with a redundant warning about the pseudo-call pattern.

\textbf{Option B — register stub geos-rag MCP server that returns "use Glob/Grep instead" for all calls (medium)}

Add a server that handles \texttt{mcp\_\_geos-rag\_\_search\_*} calls and returns a static error string. minimax's pseudo-calls would still fail to execute (they're text, not real tool\_use), but the AGENT would now see geos-rag tools in its tool list and might switch from pseudo-syntax to real \texttt{tool\_use} invocation. If it does, the stub returns "this tool is unavailable in this configuration; use Glob/Grep/Read against /geos\_lib/ instead", and the agent moves on.

This is more invasive but might also help if minimax's failure mode is "I think mcp__geos-rag__* should be in the tool list, so I'll emit a pseudo-call assuming someone routes it" rather than "I'm just emitting text". Cell semantics now differ between backbones, which complicates the cross-model story.

\textbf{Option C — register the real geos-rag MCP server, accepting that the cell becomes X+R+M (most invasive)}

Just enable RAG. The minimax X+M cell becomes X+R+M (or "X+M with stub-geos-rag for backbone-portability"). Cleanest from a "make it work" perspective; muddies the cross-model story because now minimax X+M $\neq$ DSv4 X+M.

\textbf{My recommendation: Option A}, then if that fails, Option C with explicit relabelling. Option B adds complexity without a clear win.

## Concrete plan

1. Implement Option A: add a CLI flag \texttt{--anti-pseudo-mcp-block} to \texttt{scripts/run\_experiment.py} that, when set, appends the warning block to the system prompt. Default off (preserves autocamp parity).
2. Re-run minimax × X+M × test-17 × 1 seed with the flag on, results to a new run name (\texttt{minimax\_minimax-m2.7\_F4\_antipseudo\_s1}).
3. Compare to the original $0.392$ result. If the lift is substantial (e.g. $> +20$pp), report this as the corrected cross-model number with a transparency note about the original failure mode.
4. If even Option A doesn't recover, fall back to Option C: enable RAG as a defensive measure, label it as such, and discuss the cross-model adapter-ranking caveat in the paper.

The original failed run is preserved as evidence of the pseudo-call failure mode; the corrected run gives the apples-to-apples comparison the cross-model panel is supposed to provide.
